moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Pirc Defense, a hypermodern opening where Black allows White to build a full pawn center and then attacks it from the flanks. White plays 1.e4, claiming central space. The Pirc leads to dynamic positions where White's space advantage clashes with Black's flexible piece play.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn. White plans d4 next for a commanding two-pawn center.
plans: Build a d4-e4 pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly behind the strong center
alternatives: 1.d4 — leads to queen's pawn openings, avoids the Pirc entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...d6, the Pirc Defense. Instead of immediately challenging the center, Black says: "go ahead, build your big center — I'll undermine it later." The d6 pawn prepares ...Nf6 and ...g6, setting up the fianchetto that will target White's d4 pawn from the long diagonal.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn is modest but flexible. It controls e5 and supports a later ...e5 or ...c5 break d.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure White's center from the flank
Prepare ...e5 or ...c5 to challenge the center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...e5 — the most direct reply, immediately fighting for the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White builds the maximum center with d4, establishing the e4+d4 pawn duo. Together these pawns control c5, d5, e5, and f5 — the strongest possible central formation. Against the Pirc, this imposing center is both a strength (space advantage) and a potential target (overextension).
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The classical d4-e4 center gives White significant space advantage.
plans: Develop pieces behind the center and prepare a kingside attack
Push f4 to build an even bigger center and threaten f5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, pressuring e4 and continuing the hypermodern strategy. The Nf6 is flexible — it attacks e4, controls d5, and prepares the ...g6 fianchetto. In the Pirc, the knight and the coming Bg7 work together to undermine White's center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change. Black keeps maximum flexibility for the structure.
plans: Fianchetto with ...g6 and ...Bg7 next
Castle kingside quickly and then attack the center
alternatives: 2...g6 — transposes to the same thing usually, but Nf6 first keeps options open

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, defending e4 and controlling the d5 square. The Nc3 is the backbone of White's center — it keeps e4 secure, prevents any ...d5 breaks, and supports the ambitious f4 advance in the Austrian Attack.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The center is fully supported. Nc3 makes e4 rock-solid and prepares expansive setups.
plans: Play f4 for the Austrian Attack — the most aggressive option
Develop Nf3 and Be2 for a quieter classical setup
alternatives: 3.f3 — preparing a big center with Be3 and Qd2, the Saemisch-like approach

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays g6, beginning the fianchetto that defines the Pirc. The bishop will go to g7 where it becomes the most important piece — aiming at White's d4 pawn along the a1-h8 diagonal, defending the kingside, and supporting future ...c5 or ...e5 central breaks.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The g6 pawn creates a shelter for the king and prepares the fianchetto.
plans: Complete the fianchetto with ...Bg7
Castle quickly and then look for ...e5 or ...c5 counterplay

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays f4, entering the Austrian Attack — the most aggressive system against the Pirc. The f4 pawn creates the d4+e4+f4 triple-pawn center, the most ambitious formation in chess. White aims to overwhelm Black with a direct e5 advance or a kingside pawn storm.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4-e4-f4 pawn trio is powerful but commits White's f-pawn early.
plans: Develop quickly and push e5 to kick the f6 knight and gain more space
Use the f4-f5 advance to attack Black's kingside
alternatives: 4.Nf3 — the Classical system, less aggressive but very solid

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop takes its throne on g7, the most powerful piece in the Pirc Defense. From g7, it pressures the d4 pawn along the a1-h8 diagonal, defends the kingside dark squares, and supports both ...c5 and ...e5 central breaks. The Dragon bishop of the Pirc.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change, but the bishop on g7 adds dynamic pressure to White's d4 pawn, which is the base o.
plans: Castle immediately and get the king safe
Prepare ...c5 or ...e5 to blow open the long diagonal for the bishop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, completing kingside minor piece development. The Nf3 supports d4, controls e5, and prepares kingside castling. In the Austrian Attack, the Nf3 also supports the e5 advance that White will aim for.
plans: Castle kingside and prepare e5 or f5 advances
Play Bd3 to develop the bishop actively
alternatives: 5.e5 — immediate central advance, very sharp but premature

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles immediately, getting the king to safety behind the fianchettoed Bg7. Against the Austrian Attack's aggressive setup, king safety is urgent — delaying castling would leave the king vulnerable to the e5 central break that blows the position open.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The king is safe behind the g6-f7-h7 pawns. Black's structure is compact and ready for counterplay.
plans: Play ...c5 to attack White's d4 pawn
Prepare ...e5 to challenge the center head-on

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to d3, the ideal square in the Austrian Attack. The Bd3 targets the kingside (particularly h7), supports the e4 pawn, and prepares an aggressive setup. Combined with f4, the Bd3 creates a dangerous attacking battery aimed at Black's castled king.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop supports the central pawns and prepares for kingside operations.
plans: Castle and then launch a kingside attack with e5 or f5
Use the d3-h7 diagonal for attacking chances
alternatives: 6.Be2 — safer but less aggressive, the bishop is passive on e2

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays Na6, an unusual-looking but strategically purposeful move. The knight heads for c7, from where it supports both ...b5 and the critical ...c5 break. Going to a6 instead of c6 is key — on c6, the knight would block the c-pawn, preventing Black's most important counterattack.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black preserves the ability to play ...c5, which is the most important structural break in the Pirc.
plans: Reroute the knight via c7 to support ...c5 or reach e6
Play ...c5 to challenge the d4 pawn and activate the g7 bishop
alternatives: 6...Nc6 — more natural looking but blocks the c-pawn, making ...c5 impossible

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, getting the king safe and connecting the rooks. With both sides castled on the same wing, it becomes a complex strategic battle — White wants to use the space advantage for an e5 or g4 attack, while Black targets d4 with ...c5.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. White's pawn center remains dominant and the king is safe.
plans: Play e5 to gain more space and kick the f6 knight
Push f5 to pry open Black's kingside

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes with c5, the critical counter-blow that challenges d4 — the base of White's pawn chain. This is the whole reason the knight went to a6 instead of c6. The ...c5 break opens the position and activates the Bg7, which has been waiting for this moment.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c5 pawn challenges d4 directly. If dxc5 dxc5, Black gets an open d-file and a strong bishop on.
plans: Open the long diagonal for the g7 bishop
Create counterplay on the queenside and in the center
alternatives: 7...e5 — also possible, but ...c5 is the more standard and flexible break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays d5, closing the center. This is a committal decision — the locked center reduces the Bg7's power on the long diagonal (the d4 pawn no longer hangs), but it also creates a permanent structure where both sides have clear plans: White attacks on the kingside, Black expands on the queenside.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Closed center with pawns on d5-e4-f4 vs d6-c5.
plans: Attack on the kingside with f5 or e5
Use the space advantage to build pressure
alternatives: 8.dxc5 dxc5 — opens the position, which actually favors Black's bishop on g7

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight completes its journey to c7, exactly as planned from a6. From c7, the knight supports the ...b5 queenside advance, can jump to e8-f6 for kingside defense, or reroute to a6-b4 targeting the d5 pawn. The Na6-c7 maneuver is the signature of Black's Pirc strategy.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black is preparing ...b5 and queenside counterplay.
plans: Play ...a6 and ...b5 for queenside expansion
Prepare ...e6 to challenge the d5 pawn when ready
alternatives: 8...Nc7 is essentially the only move — the knight needs to get off a6

Fight for central control with pawns and pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Classical System is an important variation of the Pirc Defence. A solid, positional approach from White.
Black replies with d6. This is the defining move of the Classical System — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
h3 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
dxe5 captures the pawn. This exchange is thematic in the Classical System — it defines the resulting pawn structure and piece activity.
dxe5 completes the variation. The resulting position after this capture offers Black clear targets and plans. Study this structure — you'll see it often.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the Pirc Defence, the Classical Qa5 Pin is a tactical pattern you should know. Black plays ...d5 at the right moment, opening the center.
In the Pirc Defence, the Austrian Attack Crush is a dangerous line you must be aware of. If you don't react precisely to the e5-e6 push, White's attack crashes through.
fxe6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
In the Pirc Defence, the 150 Attack Kingside Storm is a dangerous line you must be aware of. After the dark-squared bishops are traded, Black's king is permanently weakened.
Bxh6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
Qxh6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
